children.name: Gorilla
children.description: The largest living primates, native to the forests of central Africa. Gorillas are highly social, living in groups led by a dominant silverback male, and are primarily terrestrial herbivores. Critically endangered due to habitat loss, poaching, and disease.

children.name: Gorilla gorilla
children.description: Gorilla gorilla (Western gorilla) is a gorillas species found in Africa region.

children.name: Gorilla beringei
children.description: Gorilla beringei (Eastern gorilla) is a gorillas species found in Africa region.

children.name: Pan
children.description: Humans' closest living relatives, sharing approximately 98.7% of DNA with Homo sapiens. Pan comprises two species — the common chimpanzee and the bonobo — both confined to the forests of central Africa and distinguished by markedly different social structures.

children.name: Pan troglodytes
children.description: Pan troglodytes (Common chimpanzee) is a chimpanzees species found in Africa region.

children.name: Pan paniscus
children.description: Pan paniscus (Bonobo) is a chimpanzees species found in Africa region.

children.name: Pongo
children.description: The only great apes native to Asia, orangutans inhabit the tropical rainforests of Borneo and Sumatra. They are the most arboreal of the great apes and the most solitary, with a highly developed intelligence and exceptionally long inter-birth intervals.

children.name: Pongo pygmaeus
children.description: Pongo pygmaeus (Bornean orangutan) is a orangutans species found in Asia region.

children.name: Pongo abelii
children.description: Pongo abelii (Sumatran orangutan) is a orangutans species found in Asia region.

children.name: Pongo tapanuliensis
children.description: Pongo tapanuliensis (Tapanuli orangutan) is a orangutans species found in Asia region.

children.name: Homo
children.description: A genus of bipedal great apes with exceptionally large brains, arising in Africa roughly 2–3 million years ago. At least a dozen species are known from the fossil record, though only Homo sapiens survives today. Several archaic species — including Neanderthals and Denisovans — interbred with modern humans, leaving traces in living genomes.

children.name: Homo sapiens
children.description: Homo sapiens (Human) is a human species found in Africa and Europe and Asia and North America and South America and Oceania regions.

children.name: Homo neanderthalensis
children.description: Homo neanderthalensis (†Neanderthal) is a archaic humans species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Homo denisova
children.description: Homo denisova (†Denisovan) is a archaic humans species found in Asia region.

children.name: Homo heidelbergensis
children.description: Homo heidelbergensis (†Heidelberg man) is a archaic humans species found in Africa and Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Homo erectus
children.description: Homo erectus (†Java / Peking man) is a archaic humans species found in Africa and Asia regions.

children.name: Homo habilis
children.description: Homo habilis (†Handy man) is a archaic humans species found in Africa region.

children.name: Homo floresiensis
children.description: Homo floresiensis (†Hobbit) is a archaic humans species found in Asia region.

children.name: Homo naledi
children.description: Homo naledi (†Naledi man) is a archaic humans species found in Africa region.

children.name: Australopithecus
children.description: Extinct bipedal hominins from Africa, living roughly 2–4 million years ago. Australopithecus walked upright but retained many ape-like features including small brains and long arms. The famous fossil 'Lucy' (A. afarensis) is among the best-known human ancestors, demonstrating that upright walking preceded the evolution of large brains.

children.name: Australopithecus afarensis
children.description: Australopithecus afarensis (†Lucy's species) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.

children.name: Australopithecus africanus
children.description: Australopithecus africanus (†Taung child's species) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.

children.name: Australopithecus sediba
children.description: Australopithecus sediba (†Sediba) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.

children.name: Australopithecus anamensis
children.description: Australopithecus anamensis (†Anam man) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.

children.name: Paranthropus
children.description: The robust australopithecines — a side-branch of the human family tree with massively developed jaw muscles and large, flat molars adapted for grinding tough plant material. Despite their formidable chewing apparatus, they were not direct human ancestors but a parallel evolutionary experiment that eventually died out.

children.name: Paranthropus boisei
children.description: Paranthropus boisei (†Nutcracker man) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.

children.name: Paranthropus robustus
children.description: Paranthropus robustus (†Robust australopithecine) is a australopithecines species found in Africa region.
